pr_review_comment_body: Why don't we consolidate prefetching via the `BlockPrefetcher` with resolving blocks in parallel over multiple threads via `ray.get()`? I.e. we don't need both `prefetch_batches` and `prefetch_blocks` at the API level, and we don't need `BlockPrefetcher`/`prefetch_batches_locally`, when we can resolve (call `ray.get()`) concurrently across `prefetch_batches` threads. `prefetch_batches` would completely control the prefetching behavior, and we'd use the `batch_size` + the block metadata to know how many blocks to resolve in each thread (this is already taken care of via the bundling to create `LogicalBatch`s).

Spoke offline, the current behavior is very surprising and not very useful. In case users using the current behavior, we can't change it.

Instead I propose that we add a new `LiteralValueFilter` that
1. for ints converts them into bytestrings using the current logic
2. for strs converts them to bytestrings
3. accepts bytestrings as is
4. once everything is normalized to a bytestring, iterate over the bytes and properly escape the bytes to encure they don't get interpreted as control characters. Example: https://github.com/googleapis/java-bigtable/blob/main/google-cloud-bigtable/src/main/java/com/google/cloud/bigtable/data/v2/internal/RegexUtil.java#L53-L81
5. Deprecate ExactValueFilter and encourage people to migrate to the new filter
6. Next major version drop ExactValueFilter

Like if you pass the generator to another task, and that task calls ray.get on each inner ref but deletes the refs before it returns. > 5\. The Workflow Server can check the processing job status in the DB / Result queue. If the processing job has failed, the Workflow Server knows exactly which job failed and can resubmit the job through the Processing Server. Of course, assuming that the Workflow Server is smart enough to be able to fix the error (which is again a broad topic). Potentially, user involvement will be required here to manually submit the job after fixing the external error.

Much of the above speaks to the necessity to (somehow) mark the **reason** for failures at the message level, discerning between temporary outtime and unfixable problems.

There is also another case – at the workflow / API level: [evaluator](https://github.com/OCR-D/spec/issues/172) failures. The latter would allow triggering alternative workflow routes. Naturally, that distinction (fail vs. flop) must even be coded at the user level. So if the overall workflow has no alternative routes anymore, the user should be notified to take action (like browsing the interim results and reconfiguring the workflow).

But for the purely technical failures I don't see the user in the loop. If there is a technical problem, the admin should take action.

The main issue is that `get_options()` is a `const` method, for 2 reasons:
- It is `const` in the MMOD loss
- All the options should not be easily exposed to be changed after the network is initialized.

In fact, the only thing that makes sense to be changed is the NMS... And it is actually not used by the loss function itself, only by the `to_labels` method. I was also thinking if it shouldn't be just an extra parameter there:

``` c++
template <
            typename SUB_TYPE,
            typename label_iterator
            >
        void to_label (
            const tensor& input_tensor,
            const SUB_TYPE& sub,
            label_iterator iter,
            double adjust_threshold = 0.25,
            test_box_overlap overlaps_nms(0.45, 1),
            bool classwise = true
        ) const;
```
But I didn't like constructing a new `test_box_overlap` object by default every time this thing is called, and it's also nice to have the NMS printed inside the loss (even if it's only used for inference).

So yes, I think it's convenient to have a small helper method to change all the stuff related to the NMS.

With Python 3.9, `transform_slice` is (almost surely) no longer needed (i.e. can return its parameter unchanged) as explained below.
`transform_slice` is (only) used by `visit_Subscript`. Its purpose is to wrap subscriptions into function calls, for reading subscriptions into calls to `_getitem_`. Formerly, `Slice` was only allowed inside a `Subscript` and could not be directly used as parameter to `_getitem_`; with Python 3.9, `Slice` is a subtype of `expr` and can likely be directly used as parameter to `_getitem_`.

I suggest to check for version >= 3.9 and return in this case `slice_` unchanged and otherwise use the current code. If this approach is invalid, the tests should show this (likely with a `TypeError` when the `_getitem_` call is compiled).

Looks like you didn't edit the parser but put all logics into `FeatureUnion`s converter. I perfer to create of ONNXMLTOOLS Operator during the parsing stage. This design somehow violates my original idea: parsing stage produces IR objects and those IR objects are converted at conversion stage. Let me try to add some details about a design in my mind.
1. Go to [this function](https://github.com/onnx/onnxmltools/blob/533c877194ed2c168f61219a03a4a094f5e1cd80/onnxmltools/convert/sklearn/_parse.py#L173) and add a new `_parse_sklearn_feature_union(scope, model, inputs)`
2. An implementation of `_parse_sklearn_feature_union(scope, model, inputs)`
```python
# scope is Scope in our IR
# model is FeatureUnion
# inputs is a string list with a single element
_parse_sklearn_feature_union(scope, model, inputs):
    # Output variable name of each transform. It's a list of string.
    transformed_result_names = []
    # Encode each transform as our IR objects
    for transform in model.transformer_list:
        transformed_result_names.add(_parse_sklearn_simple_model(scope, transform, inputs)
    # Create a Concat ONNX node
    concat_operator = scope.declare_local_operator('SklearnConcat')
    concat_operator.inputs = transformed_result_names

    # Declare output name of scikit-learn FeatureUnion
    union_name = scope.declare_local_variable('union')
    concat_operator.outputs.add(union_name)

    return union_name
```
3. Create a converter for `SklearnConcat` to `onnxmltools/convert/sklearn/operator_converters/SklearnConcat.`
4. Create a shape calculator for `SklearnConcat`.

>  Each post-condition is a triple -- an asset name, a comparator, and a literal value to compare it to. I don't care to support anything else beyond this, since the whole point of this feature as I understand it is to help the principal who sends the transaction remain safe from bad contracts.

I disagree with this -- it doesn't fit the mental model of a transfer. For a real world metaphor, imagine a check: you write the amount you want to send on the check, _not_ your final balance. Other blockchains more or less all work like this -- they tell you how much you are sending, _not_ your final balance.

There's also a lot of weird behavior if your balance _changes_ due to some other transaction between when you sign and when the transaction is included in a block -- suddenly the post-condition might fail, even though the intended transaction would have been successful and safe (i.e., you have enough balance to cover the transfer, and you still want to have transferred, but the final balance is no longer equal to what you expect).

This is why I think we should be encoding a list of maximum spends, e.g., a list of triples

```
(principal, asset_type, max_amount_spent)
```

> I think I have my answer to my original question -- the purpose of the log_*() methods and the AssetMap are to prevent double-spending of assets. That we could use this information to enforce post-conditions is a serendipitous side-effect, but not the intended goal of these methods and structs

Not exactly -- the `log_*` methods exist only to encode 'transfer events' in the assetmap, which exists solely to answer questions like "How many Stacks did I transfer during the current transaction?" and "Which names did I transfer during the current transaction?" -- that can be used to enforce the "transfer limits" on transactions, but it won't prevent double-spends (that's prevented by the balance/ownership checks in `assets.rs`)

### Size filter

Discussion history:

> **mmckerns:** Another interesting filter might be very large objects...
> 
> **leogama:** This is an even harder problem. `sys.getsizeof()` gives reasonable values for some objects, like an array, but not others, like a list of arrays or a complex object from `scikit-learn`. What I can think of is 1) to "`dumps`" the object to a bytes string; 2) check the object's serialized size; 3) discard it or write the bytes directly to the stream, taking care of the module's dictionary opcodes manually as in the unpickable case or wrapping it in a lightweight dill class that would "`loads`" it at unpickling.
> 
> **mmckerns:** Arrays work here because they are containers of ctypes. Lists and other python containers hold references, so the lists themselves are small, but some of the referenced items are not. I think it would be fair to: (1) `getsizeof` an object, and replace anything that registers as bigger than some cutoff with a stub, and/or (2) pickle an object to a byte string as you suggest, then make a decision based on the resultant size. The latter might be a slow process... where filtering by size, especially in the latter case, is going to be inefficient. You already do something similar to `getsizeof` in the trace, I can imagine that it could be leveraged in a filter.

```python
import os
import signal
import threading
import time

* The value provided to `nice` is an increment. Search for `int nice (int increment) ` in the [glibc docs](https://www.gnu.org/software/libc/manual/pdf/libc.pdf)

@kgryte I'm not as sure about the wider JS world, but I do know that taking advantage of `&&` short-circuiting is part of the idiom of Python (it's discussed extensively even in introductory tutorials), it's part of the idiom of React, and it's also used frequently in jlab core. Whether you change to using it here or not is a minor stylistic (and maybe performance?) niggle, but in general I don't think we have any reason to shy away from using the short-circuiting syntax/semantics.

I think you can reason about the behavior if you remember two things:

- Most (all?) values in Python/JS can participate in logical operations, but they won't be automagically converted to booleans

- The point of short-circuiting is to not evaluate the second clause of a logical operator if the answer is known after evaluating the first

> asked the result type of `'' && null`

From the above, you can work out that since `''` is falsey, the second clause will be ignored and the return value will just be `''`. 

Another tricky question would be "what's the return value of `'' || null`?". I'll try to prove my point by working it out (since I don't actually know the answer): `''` is falsey, so no short circuiting happens here, the second clause will be eval-ed, and the return will be `null`.

I mean that the returned type from `fft(some_real_matrix)` would encode the fact that it holds a packed representation.  So that then `ifftr()` wouldn't be required.  You would just do `ifft()` and it would know it was a packed representation.  It's complicated though because someone is going to do stuff to the complex packed matrix, like transform it somehow and the fact that it's packed would need to be propagated all the way through all that too.  I can think of a bunch of ways to go at that problem in the typesystem. I'm not sure what would actually not just end up irritating users though.  

The simplest thing I can think of is to just have a new complex type like `dlib::packed_complex<T>` or something like that.  Then the output of a real fft would be something like `dlib::matrix<dlib::packed_complex<double>>` and `ifft(dlib::matrix<dlib::packed_complex<double>>)` would know to do the packed version.  But now there is this other complex type which is definitely going to be irritating to users.

Anyway, none of the ways to do it are worth it and probably all are confusing to users.

### Pseudo code
My understanding of how training would work (as the spec is currently written) is below (in pseudo python code). I'm trying to emphasise the details that we are discussing, but this should be equivalent to any implementation in terms of results.
```python
def bind(bindings, src, dst):
    for (k,v) in bindings.items():
        dst[k].value = src[v].value
def run(graph, constants, inputs, callable={}):
    """Compute the graph with the given inputs and constants.
    The 'graph' can contain GraphCall to graphs in 'callable'.
    Does NOT mutate graph/constants/inputs."""
    return ...

# Load the model. The model is conceptually trained to some point (0 or more iterations).
model = onnx.load(session.model_path)
# Unlike for inference, we don't allow constant folding of trainable constants
optimize(model, constant_folding_blacklist=trainable_initializers(model))

# Initialize pretty much the same way as for inference.
# I'm explicitly deleting the initializers here to show we are referring only to the
# constant buffers in all graph evaluations henceforth.
# Similarly, these constant buffers could be attached to the graph, I'm making
# them explicit to hopefully make it clearer when they do and don't change.
graph_constants = create_buffers_from(model.graph.initializer)
del model.graph.initializer
algo_constants = [create_buffers_from(info) for info in model.training_info]
for info in model.training_info:
    del info.algorithm.initializer

# Training intialization process (if starting training from scratch);
# overwrites constants.
if session.train_from_scratch:
    for (i, info) in enumerate(model.training_info):
        # Evaluate the initialization graph, which might include generating random
        # tensors. No inputs, and no visibility of initializers outside of itself.
        outputs = run(
            graph = info.initialization,
            constants = create_buffers_from(info.initialization.initializer),
            inputs = [],
            callable = {},
        )
        # Copy the outputs of the initialization graph to the constants referenced
        # via initialization_binding.
        bind(
            bindings = info.initialization_binding,
            src = outputs,
            dst = graph_constants + algo_constants[i],
        )

# Training process
# The spec doesn't specify how data is consumed, but I'll assume we use one batch per
# training info per iteration. Not sure how iterations/training_infos interact.
data = session.get_data_generator()
for iteration in range(session.training_iterations):
    for (i, info) in enumerate(model.training_info):
        # Run the algorithm graph, with access to the inference graph constants
        # and the ability to call the inference graph with a GraphCall node.
        outputs = run(
            graph = info.algorithm,
            constants = graph_constants + algo_constants[i],
            inputs = {**next(data)},
            callable = {
                model.graph.name: (lambda inputs:
                    run(model.graph, graph_constants, inputs, callable={})
                )
            },
        )
        # Copy the outputs of the algorithm graph to the constants referenced
        # via update_binding.
        bind(
            bindings = info.update_bindings,
            src = outputs,
            dst = graph_constants + algo_constants[i],
        )

# Save the model now it has been trained for more iterations
model.graph.initializer = create_initializers_from(graph_constants)
for (i, info) in enumerate(model.training_info):
    info.algorithm.initializer = create_initializers_from(algo_constants[i])
onnx.save(model, session.out_path)
```
Note the constants are updated atomically after each evaluation of a graph -- each evaluation always sees the latest set of constants, but they don't change until after the graph has been evaluated.

As I understand the spec, the result of training should be equivalent to doing this for each iteration:
1. Load model
2. Run algorithm (equivalent to freezing inference graph as an operator and then running algorithm with GraphCall nodes replaced with that operator)
3. Update initializer values from algorithm output based on update_binding
4. Save model

As soon as you've done one iteration the values of the previous iteration are inaccessible (unless you explicitly kept them, or they aren't trainable).

Thanks for the change! Initially I thought this would work, but after testing it internally a couple of issues came up. Specifically:

1) Calling decorated_fn here can break scoping. In order to apply config scopes to references (as in "@some_scope_name/ConfigurableClass()"), the configurable version of the function gets wrapped by yet another function that applies the scope. This happens when constructing a ConfigurableReference instance here, and reuses all the same decoration mechanisms (including subclassing for classes) as regular gin.configurable decoration does:

  https://github.com/google/gin-config/blob/484d03ab6f52c05455adb06541e9d8d0189d6967/gin/config.py#L301

  So for scoped configurable references, there are two layers of wrapping, one to apply the scope, and one to supply the configured parameters. Unfortunately, for that outer layer of wrapping, calling __new__ first before calling decorated_fn means that __new__ is called before the config_scope is opened (since that happens inside decorated_fn). That inner __new__ call then in turn calls its own decorated_fn (which is responsible for supplying parameters), but since it's not in the right scope the wrong parameters can be supplied. Hopefully that's somewhat clear...

2) The second issue is that this breaks for classes that use metaclasses, when the metaclass overrides __call__ and calls __init__ internally. One fairly broadly used example of this is in Sonnet v2's ModuleMetaclass here

 
 https://github.com/deepmind/sonnet/blob/5a465696383f967d5bffb6599347d9e6c15cef4b/sonnet/src/base.py#L126

  Here's a simple test case that fails for that reason (requires Sonnet):

  ```
import sonnet.v2 as snt

configurable_linear = gin.external_configurable(snt.Linear)

...

def testConfigurableSonnetModule(self):
    config_str = """
      Linear.output_size = 100
    """
    gin.parse_config(config_str)
    linear = configurable_linear()
    self.assertEqual(linear.output_size, 100)
```

Unfortunately I think those issues are hard to fix using the current approach... I haven't yet had a chance to think through alternative implementations in any depth, so not sure what other approaches might be feasible. Do you have any thoughts on another way to address this? (One approach that I'd like to get to is to replace a lot of this quite complicated ad-hoc decoration logic with wrapt, which would certainly change a lot of things implementation wise, but it's not clear to me it would fix the pickling issue specifically.)

I still think its okay to cache at the level of "have we seen all these requests together" because rules will always emit the same MultiPlatformEPR's (for a given options config), so seeing the same request combo in the cache key amounts to caching at the rule level, which is what we want right? It seems like an anti-pattern to have two rules that emit the same EPRs, but that is the only case where caching individual requests within a MultiPlatformEPR would result in additional cache hits. If that isn't correct and we expect multiple rules to yield the same ExecuteProcessRequests, then it could make sense to cache individual request.

>Open question as to whether we should write to the cache for each of the digests, or just for the one that actually ran...

If we do change to your suggestion of a cache lookup per sub-request in a MPEPR, then I think we should write the result to the cache for *each* request in the MPEPR. I am hesitant to add a mechanism that feeds information about which request actually ran back to the caller from a CommandRunner because it would require such a large change to the way the command runners are structured.

What the echo example gets wrong is that those requests produce different output depending on platform. The output shouldn't be used interchangeably in any case. I talked with @stuhood about this issue for a while and we agreed that rules should be responsible for making sure that *if* they output multiple requests for cross-platform support that the output is interchangeable, i.e all requests in a MultiPlatformEPR are configured to to produce the same output regardless of which one is selected based on the available platform. Assuming that is true then we can use whatever output was produced for a MPEPR as the cache value for any request in the MPEPR, if we choose to cache individually.

While I like the idea of a `BlockHashMap` in general, I don't think this implementation will fly as-is.  The reason is that it makes it all but impossible for a verifier to work without access to the prover's block map.  Is there a way the block identifiers can be deterministically generated from a _set_ of block hashes?  Or, possibly, a tree of block/parent hash pairs?  If so, then this would make it possible for a light client to independently calculate the same `BlockHashMap` as a full node, using just the block headers (which is all it has access to).  This will be necessary for the light client, since it needs the block map to walk and verify the MARF Merkle proofs.

In addition, e.g. configuring a GnuPG home does not always mean you also do not want to use the agent, as you theoretically still can.

Given all this, I added a separate option flag that always has the intent to disable the agent (hence it being a `struct{}` and not a `bool`), but can be configured separately from injecting a GnuPG home. This still allows to e.g. use the environment variable to configure the home, while disabling the agent.

I am open to other suggestions, or making it e.g. boolean based. But I think detachment of this option from the GnuPG home one is good.

If we merge this PR here, the action will be enabled for any push to a branch or fork of this repo. No user furthermore has to do anything for it.

So when someone forks, commits some changes, and opens a PR, they will see Travis starting to run tests on their latest commit, but also (if need be), the black action will add another commit to their PR for fixing the format and that commit also triggers Travis/CI.

The important part: no contributor has to be made aware or needs to know how to run black on their code. That will happen automatically.

There are 2 places which would be immediate, one at the receiver `[_wait_for_response]`(https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/1f9192a3bc3880edf9b44cc23e1cd3914b8b9b0b/sdk/eventhub/azure-eventhub/azure/eventhub/_pyamqp/receiver.py#L79) , and the other at the sender by calling `[_check_if_closed]`(https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/1f9192a3bc3880edf9b44cc23e1cd3914b8b9b0b/sdk/eventhub/azure-eventhub/azure/eventhub/_pyamqp/sender.py#L164)

I think the above two flows are also slightly different due to the confusion between `is_closed` and detached. The errors would be raised here as they try to perform a send or receive.

Another way errors in `self._error` would be raised is via `link.get_state` which is called in `[client.py]`(https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/1f9192a3bc3880edf9b44cc23e1cd3914b8b9b0b/sdk/eventhub/azure-eventhub/azure/eventhub/_pyamqp/client.py#L550) ( and is part of the bigger while loop of `do_work` and  `client_ready` in the same file )

### Flow impact

`self._error` at the link level only gets populated `_incoming_detach` on line 232. After this the link can be in two states 
Detached or Error (for this case point self._error is populated)

both of these states are in the `if state in return` block of detach. They would never raise there.

if a link is any `ATTACH*` or `DETACH_SENT` state ( current implementation), `self._error` would not have anything. 

Further, in Attach state `is_closed` would be False and would break out from `check_if_closed`.

Detach_sent would be only time `is_closed` is true, but thats what would cause the `Link Error on Detach` to trigger, which is incorrect, we dont have any information from the service yet.  

So currently `check_if_closed` being called here would have an effect for 1 state. 

with the new change, if state is DETACH_SENT, no need to send a detach and at some point a detach would come from the service with any errors.

> So Member is read from DCS during dcs.touch_member

No, touch_member() write member key. Reading happens somewhere inside in get_cluster().

> and if it's different to data passed into it, then the data constructed in ha.touch_member is written (looking at the etcd3 implementation)

This is unrelated and just implementation detail of very specific DCS, because the member key is connected with the session and will not expire while the session exists. Therefore we can skip the update if value didn't changed.
But, with for example etcd v2 the member key is updated every time.

> Do you mean someone who wrote to the DCS /member/member key outside of Patroni? Who would do that? And why would that be considered something to honour?

Exactly, something different from Patroni could change arbitrary keys in DCS and we better be resilient to it.

> Also, why doesn't touch_member create a Member instance instead of constructing a Dict?

Because touch_member() only updates one key that represents current node in DCS. When we read the cluster state we are reading **all** member keys, which happens on all nodes.

* index is known.

Yes we had extended discussions about this on our end. You can see this comment for 

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/1a6f1d816d1615fb2a9d4bd8627e597b4df4920c/torch/csrc/utils/pybind.h#L380-L387

> Then it's only to address the teardown situation, which really shouldn't be that hard to clean up

It is not that trivial on our end as we have some Python object held by C++ structures that can very often outlive the python runtime (but are not necessarily global). For example some C++ cache are still populated and hold onto python object until after the python runtime dies. In that case, it is simpler to make our objects smoothly handle the case where they outlive the Python runtime rather than adding new indirections in these C++ objects to be able to check python runtime state (when it is available at runtime).

I think more generally, this happens because we're trying to discourage usage of `PyObject*` or any manual refcounting to reduce bugs in our code. In particular having temporary global states that are non-owning are dangerous as we can easily forget to do a manual decref when replacing the current field.

Since LightGBM no longer supports Python 2 (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4089#discussion_r620355287), it isn't necessary to explicitly declare that a class inherits from `object`. We have a preference for not using `(object)` in class declarations. For example, see these other class declarations in this file:

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/32fec820607d8cbcd1fe37c200455ea45702df77/python-package/lightgbm/basic.py#L1047

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/32fec820607d8cbcd1fe37c200455ea45702df77/python-package/lightgbm/basic.py#L2150

So this fixes subclasses that override `__nonzero__` and forget `__bool__`, but keeps subclasses that override `__bool__` and forget `__nonzero__` broken.

I suppose the argument is that subclasses that override `__nonzero__` and don't touch `__bool__` are more likely due to legacy Python-2-only code than subclasses that override `__bool__` and forget about `__nonzero__`.

Thread ids are always returned in the same order, in the sense that repeated calls to `get_thread_ids()` will always produce the same list. However, thread ids in child processes will necessarily be different from the thread ids of the parent, because children do not inherit threads from the parent. Therefore I test `chthreads != parent_threads` (it is allowed, though very unlikely, for some thread ids to accidentally be the same in 2 processes, but for all of them to be same is practically impossible). 
The standard does guarantee that all thread ids in a single process are unique, so there is no point in using `Counter`.

Description is added in quantize_v2.cc:
```
NNVM_REGISTER_OP(_contrib_quantize_v2)
  .describe(R"code(Quantize a input tensor from float to `out_type`,
  with user-specified `min_calib_range` and `max_calib_range` or the input range collected at runtime.
  
  Output `min_range` and `max_range` are scalar floats that specify the range for the input data.
  
  When out_type is `uint8`, the output is calculated using the following equation:
  
  `out[i] = (in[i] - min_range) * range(OUTPUT_TYPE) / (max_range - min_range) + 0.5`,
  
  where `range(T) = numeric_limits<T>::max() - numeric_limits<T>::min()`.
  
  When out_type is `int8`, the output is calculate using the following equation
  by keep zero centered for the quantized value:
  
  `out[i] = sign(in[i]) * min(abs(in[i] * scale + 0.5f, quantized_range)`,
  
  where
  `quantized_range = MinAbs(max(int8), min(int8))` and
  `scale = quantized_range / MaxAbs(min_range, max_range).`
  
  When out_type is `auto`, the output type is automatically determined by min_calib_range if presented.
  If min_calib_range < 0.0f, the output type will be int8, otherwise will be uint8.
  If min_calib_range isn't presented, the output type will be int8.
  
  .. Note::
      This operator only supports forward propogation. DO NOT use it in training.)code" ADD_FILELINE)
```

In general TF provides a 3 week window for forward compatibility, per https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/compat/forward_compatible
The branch ensures that the HEAD of python produces GraphDefs that an earlier version of serving infra can still execute.
It doesn't check against the date of the system, but there is a date stamp baked into TensorFlow source code that is updated daily.

We remove these forward compatible branches (long) after the window expires. That's why they don't show up frequently in the code search. Here are a few left overs:

I looked into the `method` meaning. Basically, it's a convenience to communicate a polygon (or simple square) in lieu of a list of points. In MATLAB, a polygon or square is gridded at some resolution to create a list of points that cover the area, after which the algorithm is the same as if `discrete` is provided.

My vote here is to remove `method` and the kwargs supporting the 'square' option (`capacity` and `density`). In a future PR we can implement a public function `prepare_wvm_inputs` that accepts the simpler inputs (e.g. a polygon) and returns the list of grid points. That keeps the `wvm` function with a single purpose: compute the smoothed clearsky index using the wavelet transform over a grid of points.

>>> sum + val1 + val2
inf
>>> sum64 = np.float64(3e38)

3e+38

> the docs page just keeps reloading in a loop https://owlish.imbstack.com/docs/

Here's what I think is happening:

nginx is not serving `docs.html` when the user types `https://owlish.imbstack.com/docs` in the URL bar. It's serving `index.html`. `index.html` has a [react-router route](https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster-web/blob/ade83943bdf93957f8e55c30c9dfd62e0a4cc536/src/App/routes.jsx#L6-L10) which tries to switch entry points whenever the user navigates to a route from `index.html` that starts with `/docs` (for example clicking a link in tc-web that should navigate the user to the docs site). This calls the [SwitchEntryPoint](https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster-web/blob/ade83943bdf93957f8e55c30c9dfd62e0a4cc536/src/views/SwitchEntryPoint/index.jsx) component which reloads the page but since nginx fails to route to `docs.html` after reloading, it continuously refreshes the page.

Because there are very select places that we want _all_ dependencies, both normal and special cased. Specifically, project introspection needs _all_ dependencies. 

It is simpler in those places to have one call `await Get(Addresses, DependenciesRequest)` than a call for normal deps + call for all the special cased. There are two designs that can be expressed by the user:

```py
invite.target_user_type is InviteTargetUser.stream
```

or, the easier to read implementation given here:

```py
invite.is_stream_invite()
```

The first design is more explicit towards how the Discord API functions but the second one is admittedly a lot more user friendly. The second design could replace the first design entirely and we could completely forego the Enum approach, storing an integer directly within the model. I think for both documentation reasons and ease of implementability, the second design with this interface is probably better.

I think you need to check something about the event instead of assuming the job is done if it doesn't have any conditions. For one thing, we might be getting "added" or "deleted" events about new jobs being created or old jobs being successfully destroyed, not just "updated" events. For another, we can get an "updated" event when the job undergoes any change, not just when it finishes or fails.

We should ignore any events that aren't "updated" events, and we should ignore any events where the job isn't actually done running. I think you might be seeing jobs without `jobObject.status.conditions` set because the job is not really done yet.

You should check `jobObject.status.finished` and `jobObject.status.failed`. A job that is actually finished will have a nonzero value for one of those. Pending and running jobs will have 0 for both.

The default value would be anything <= the value of `--keep-last-params`. 

The reason for adding the arg `--cache-last-best-params` in addition to `--keep-last-params' is because the semantics are different. `--keep-last-params'  does just what it says, makes sure that we keep only the last N params files we saved. However, these last N params files in time are not necessarily the last N **best** params files, and very often are not. If we don't make the effort to keep the N **best** params files, then if we want to build an averaged params file, we will not be able to use the true N best. 

Now, we could just set `--keep-last-params' to zero, and keep every params file we save off. But this can eat an awful lot of disk space. This change makes sure we keep the information we need to build an accurate averaged parameter set, and to save disk space while so doing.

There is no great reason for using 'cache' instead of 'keep'. It seemed more evocative of what this is trying to do, and to distinguish of `--keep-last-params`.

Locally I am using mypy to typecheck this module and try to catch any potential bug via static analysis. It turns out that the way Python works a logical expression does not necessarily returns a boolean value. Due to the short-circuit mechanisms it can return anything. In the case, without the `bool()`, the type of this expression is `Union[List[str], bool]`.

In practice, this does not really matters, the code works with or without `bool()` in the same way. But, if I leave the `bool()` there it means that I can keep relying on `mypy` to catch other bugs.

(In the future, I plan to suggest for setuptools to start incrementally adopting type hints and checks, in a module by module basis. `setuptools.discovery` would be ready for that).

We need to decide here if we want BC or not. You can't adopt this proposal without offering a new API that achieves a minimum BC-level. Personally I don't favour the verbosity that this brings and how trivial some of the transforms become. For example what's the use of `HorizontalFlip` if it always flips? Why not use a lambda + the underlying kernel? You can argue that this would be even more composable and have even less classes to maintain.

Personally I think to get the maximum chances of rolling out the transforms successfully and getting adoption is to offer straight replacements of the existing classes and limited-time support for newly deprecated classes (with clear messaging on how to move out of them). Think of it as having a limited budget of breakages you can make. Would you spend it on RandomHorizontalFlip or on fixing the ToTensor and eliminating the use of ConvertImageDtype? I would definitely choose the latter.

> `Add a zvmguestconfigure service file for ubuntu, add 'DefaultDependencies=no' to remove cycle depends on sysinit with cloud-init-local;`
> 
> is this special requirement only by ubuntu 20? any reference on the reason ? systemd version updated?

This is because the cloud-init20.1-10 which installed by default in ubuntu20. The cloud-init-local.service has dependency 'Before=sysinit.target':
```
dummyuser@u0616:/lib/systemd/system$ cat cloud-init-local.service
[Unit]
Description=Initial cloud-init job (pre-networking)
DefaultDependencies=no
Wants=network-pre.target
After=systemd-remount-fs.service
Before=NetworkManager.service
Before=network-pre.target
Before=shutdown.target
Before=sysinit.target
Conflicts=shutdown.target
RequiresMountsFor=/var/lib/cloud
```

If zvmguestconfigure do not add 'DefaultDependencies=no', it will have dependency for 'sysinit.target' by default, which is a cycle.

```
dummyuser@ubuntu20image:~$ systemctl list-dependencies zvmguestconfigure.service

* get data from the database
* possibly transform it into something that makes sense for our application (rounding does not count as such, but statistics would *if* we don't store the underlying data -- for instance, you don't store the mean value of a series along with the series itself, otherwise the statistics may get inconsistent with actual data)
* store raw data in a live object
* render data from that object in a view, where "render" can mean rounding, simple calculation, etc.

When sampling from a replay buffer, we choose which experiences to train our agent with. A straightforward strategy that has proven effective for many algorithms is to pick these
samples uniformly at random.
A more advanced strategy (proven better in many cases) is `Prioritized Experiences Replay (PER) <https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.05952>`__.
In PER, single items in the buffer are assigned a (scalar) priority value, which denotes their significance, or in simpler terms, how much we expect
to learn from these items. Experiences with a higher priority are more likely to be sampled.

> It seems that `inited` is reset to `False` only when early stopping is not triggered?

I believe `inited` is reset to `False` when early stopping is triggered here
https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/dff622c26aceb5bc602a6279d4a45d7e99d389e5/python-package/lightgbm/callback.py#L339-L340
Triggering early stopping means to raise `EarlyStopException` and `inited` is reset to `False` right before that line.

Also, if it is not reset, the test `test_grid_search()` will fail because we use constant custom evaluation metric there which force early stopping to happen at `stopping_rounds` iteration because there is no improvement after the first iteration.

Here is a flow actions which I think may be problematic with the changes in this PR:

- `SetCslPeriod()` can be called at any point of time (user can call `otLinkCslSetPeriod()` at any point).
- So it can be called while we are in middle of a frame tx (`SubMac` is in tx state).
- `SetCslPeriod()` will call the newly added `Mac::CslSample()`.
- From `Mac::CslSample()` we can end up calling `mLinks.GetSubMac().CslSample();` (e.g. if period is changed)
- `SubMac::CslSample()` then calls `SubMac::HandleCslTimer()`
- `HandleCslTimer()` will only look at csl state and may call `Get<Radio>().Sleep())` 
- So I guess we can end up putting radio to sleep in middle of an ongoing tx.

The issue is that the statically-linked OpenCL ICD loader is not able to load the AMD App SDK driver that we use for testing on the CPU. The call to `dlopen` fails with the error `cannot load any more object with static TLS`. This seems to only happen with the AMD App SDK. I have tested with an NVIDIA GPU and with the PoCL CPU implementation and it worked fine with both.

When running under Python 3.8 on Ubuntu 14.04, the AMD App SDK driver fails to load immediately. With Python 3.7, it only fails to load if the `dask` tests run first. I isolated that by running various combinations and orders of tests.

The whole issue seems to come from the AMD App SDK driver using statically allocated thread local storage (STATIC_TLS), which means that the amount of TLS objects is limited, and the library loading order can influence when the limit gets reached.

It's worth noting that both neither the NVIDIA OpenCL driver, nor the PoCL one use STATIC_TLS, so the wheel works fine with them under various versions of Python. I do not have access to the required hardware, but I have checked that the various ICDs available on Ubuntu 20.04 (Mesa, Beignet, and Intel) do not use STATIC_TLS either.

```
nodeInfoResponse {

This simplifies the logic on the front-end a lot, since trying to refactor the log and error features to work with the `NodeFeatures` and `WorkerFeatures` arguments would have been difficult otherwise.

Dear @mfussenegger, @matriv, and @seut,

regarding @mfussenegger's proposal to let the user optionally specify a time zone, and make the machinery return a timezone-aware datetime object, I see two options here.

Let's assume the user defines a custom timezone object like
```python
>>> import datetime
>>> tz_mst = datetime.timezone(datetime.timedelta(hours=7), name="MST")
```

and a timestamp in Epoch like
```python
# Fri, 16 Sep 2022 11:09:16 GMT
timestamp = 1663326556
```

On this spot, which variant would be semantically the right one? Note that [`datetime.utcfromtimestamp`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp) does not accept the `tz` keyword argument, it will always return a naive datetime object with `tzinfo=None`.

1. We can either use [`datetime.fromtimestamp`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp), which **does** accept the `tz` keyword argument.
   ```python
   >>> datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(timestamp, tz=tz_mst)
   datetime.datetime(2022, 9, 16, 18, 9, 16, tzinfo=datetime.timezone(datetime.timedelta(seconds=25200), 'MST'))
   ```

2. Or we can replace the `tzinfo` on the naive datetime object returned by `utcfromtimestamp`, making it timezone-aware.
   ```python
   >>> datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(timestamp).replace(tzinfo=tz_mst)
   datetime.datetime(2022, 9, 16, 11, 9, 16, tzinfo=datetime.timezone(datetime.timedelta(seconds=25200), 'MST'))
   ```

I think option 1. is the right choice. So the implementation would look like something along the lines of:
```python

def set_timezone(self, tz):
  if not isinstance(tz, datetime.timezone):
    raise TypeError(f"Timezone object has wrong type: {tz}")
  self.timezone = tz

def _to_datetime(self, value):
  """
  Convert CrateDB's `TIMESTAMP` column to the native Python `datetime` object.
  When a timezone is given, return a timezone-aware object.
  Otherwise, return a naive object which is meant to be the time in the UTC time zone.
  """
  if self.timezone is not None:
    return datetime.fromtimestamp(value / 1e3, tz=self.timezone)
  else:
    return datetime.utcfromtimestamp(value / 1e3)
```

What do you think?

With kind regards,
Andreas.

> It looks like the taskvine executor looks up the path for poncho_package_run, stages it, but I'm unsure if it actually gets used? Does the packages environment get launched differently in taskvine?

Yes and yes.

In WQ `poncho_package_run` is run every time a task launches to create an environment so it's expensive. TaskVine separates environment creation from task execution so executions can share environments. An environment creation is now a mini-task, of which output (in this case, the environment) is sharable between tasks.

`poncho_package_run` is declared as a TaskVine file in line 824, the mini-task (env creation) is declared if possible in line 868 (`declare_poncho`), and both of these TaskVine files are bound to the task in lines 873-875 (`add_environment` and `add_input`).

So this will break the current Arrow --> NumPy conversion semantics when `columns = None`, which is currently:
- Return a single NumPy ndarray for a single-column table.
- Return a dict of NumPy ndarray for a multi-column table.

This PR would change it to always return a dict of NumPy ndarray, even when the column name is our internal `TENSOR_COL_NAME`, which should definitely be returned as a single NumPy ndarray.

IMO we should align to the `_convert_batch_type_to_numpy()` semantics when `columns: List[str]` and `columns = None`, which are:
- Return a single NumPy ndarray for a single-column table with column name `TENSOR_COL_NAME`.
- Return a dict of NumPy ndarray otherwise.

This PR also currently changes the `columns: str` case, which should always return a single NumPy ndarray but with this PR would return a dict of ndarrays.

Overall, I think that the semantics should be:
- `columns = None or columns = List[str]`: `_convert_batch_type_to_numpy()` semantics.
- `columns = str`: Always return a single NumPy ndarray.

```python
        if columns is None:
            columns = self._table.column_names
        # Should add this helper to ArrowBlock.
        self._check_has_columns(columns)
        if not isinstance(columns, list):
            batch = self._table.column(columns)
        else:
            batch = self._table.select(columns)
        return _convert_batch_type_to_numpy(batch)
```

Also, we should make this same change in semantics + use of `_convert_batch_type_to_numpy()` in [`PandasBlock.to_numpy()`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/d527ce8b7d6367f83292e5da02fc3ebe6f11860a/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L193-L213).

> Currently, an `x_sources` target generator needs to be written once, and will keep up to date as more `x` files are added to a directory. Using a `TargetFilesGenerator` that subsets the outputs from another target would mean the `BUILD` file would need to be updated whenever the contents of the target would change. Admittedly, this would guarantee the best dependency/cache performance, however it would come at the expense of a maintenance burden of the build file, and the build errors that arise due to files not being captured from the outputs would be hard to debug.

I am not proposing that this integration would require explicitly listing the files to generate targets for, as I don't think that that buys you a significant difference in performance. > In my view, dependency inference is a nice-to-have for this use case, but not essential: in the `experimental_shell_command`/`experimental_run_in_sandbox` use cases, users have to specify dependencies manually.

To be clear: this is about both the dependencies of the `wrapped_as_` target, and dependencies _on_ the `wrapped_as_` target.

So in the async cluster (and only in the async code) we have this additional argument: 'connection_error_retry_attempts', which is mutually exclusive with 'retry'. 
Our guide line is to keep backward compatibility, and since the default value of [connection_error_retry_attempts is set to 5](https://github.com/redis/redis-py/blob/6b3e0b491c4a348ca2e7a332e6f3b23a5da3c461/redis/asyncio/cluster.py#L217), we need to try to maintain this default retry behavior. 
There are couple of options I thought of to maintain the same behavior, but none of them isn't great:
The first is to leave the default value as is and raise exceptions when mutually exclusive arguments passed together:
```
        if retry and retry_on_error:
            raise DataError("'retry' cannot be passed along with "
                            "'retry_on_error'. "
                            "To control these variables pass a "
                            "Retry object set with the required retries "
                            "and/or errors.")
        if retry and retry._retries != connection_error_retry_attempts:
            raise DataError("Retry attempts set in the passed 'retry' object do not match "
                            "'connection_error_retry_attempts'."
                            "'connection_error_retry_attempts' should be set to None or to "
                            "the number of retries set in the Retry object.")
```
However, as connection_error_retry_attempts == 5 by default, if someone will create a new async cluster instance and pass a retry object it will require him to set connection_error_retry_attempts to None/the same retries set in the retry object. e.g.:
```
r = RedisCluster(host, port, retry=Retry(backoff(), 4), connection_error_retry_attempts=None)
```
which obviously doesn't look good.
To solve it, we can change the connection_error_retry_attempts default value to None/0 by default, but then users that were used to get 5 retries will now have none. Do you think that it's something we can do if we'll announce about this change in the release notes?
Another option is to allow passing retry object with different retry attempts than in connection_error_retry_attempts and ignore the later when retry is passed. We can add a documentation of that, or even log a warning stating that we ignored connection_error_retry_attempts, but I don't think it would be readable at all for the user. 

WDYT? do you have a better option?

```

The `entry_start`, `entry_stop` data does not need to come from

```python
        entry_start, entry_stop = _regularize_entries_start_stop(
            ttree.num_entries, None, None
        )
```

Since we're doing whole TTrees, `entry_start = 0` and `entry_stop = ttree.num_entries`. A TTree's `num_entries` is already a lookup; creating a new lookup table for it won't optimize anything. (Though removing the `_regularize_entries_start_stop` could save a bit of time.)

It's also not necessary to have fine-tuned `step_size` per file. They differ by file because each file has slightly different numbers of items per entry, but a set of ROOT files almost always represents an [independent and identically distributed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independent_and_identically_distributed_random_variables) dataset, so the `step_size` of each file will be roughly equal. Also, chunk sizes don't need to be exact, since their purpose is to protect from memory issues on one side and overhead on the other, which is an order-of-magnitude thing.

So you can average the `step_size` values of all the files, round it to the nearest integer and ensure that it's at least 1. (Probably not necessary because the `step_size` of each file is already guaranteed to be at least 1, so their average will be as well.) The `step_size` for a single file is already an average over TBaskets.

Yeah I should have explained my reasoning a bit more here. The basic idea is that in most callsites, we have an input of type `Optional[PartitionsDefinition]`. There are many categories this `partitions_def` can be in. Either:

- None
- TimeWindowPartitionsDefinition
- MultiPartitionsDefinition (exactly one time window dimension)
- MultiPartitionsDefinition (not exactly one time window dimension)
- Other PartitionsDefinition (static/dynamic)

Now, in any callsite that accepts a parameter of type PartitionsDefinition, where we want to treat "timey" partitions differently, we'll need to have two separate conditionals, one to check if it's a regular TimeWindowPartitionsDefinition, and another to check if it's a MultiPartitionsDefinition with exactly one time dimension.

Once we have validated if we're in a "timey" case, then we often want to pass that "timey" partitions def into another function. In this case, we're doing that for `_sort_key_for_time_window_partition`, but we also do a similar thing in [this function](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/dagster-io/dagster/-/blob/python_modules/dagster/dagster/_core/definitions/asset_reconciliation_sensor.py?L165), for example. In each of these cases, we need to include extra logic to disambiguate between the TimeWindowPartitionsDefinition and MultiPartitionsDefinitions, as even if they share some properties, there are some parts which are different (e.g. in the sort key case, we have to pull out the partition key in a slightly different way, even if we get the `fmt` in the same way)

So this would all be a bit simpler if we could just have `_sort_key_for_time_window_partition` just take in a TimeWindowPartitionsDefinition object directly, regardless of if it came from a MultiPartitionsDef or not.

This also helps prevent mistakes where we accidentally pass in a MultiPartitionsDefinition that does not have exactly one time dimension, by letting us rely on the static type checker.

> f) It should persist the user's dismissal of the notification, using the dates of the last sync and the last removed download

So I think this connects with this deliverable above that I mentioned.

There are two primary situations where we want this notification to show:
1) The device has insufficient storage
2) User's downloads were removed

I think the text of the notification should show in that priority: if both are true, (1) should show. Either way, the notification should be dismissable. We can track the user's dismissal in the browser's local storage (see this [Vue-use composable](https://vueuse.org/core/useLocalStorage/)). So when we query the API via `useUserSyncStatus`, we should compare what's stored in local storage with those details. Specifically, we can utilize the dates of the last sync and the last removed download to know whether the user has dismissed the notification or not.

Personally I prefer to infer the version number from `git tag` since then you can guarentee that the tag and the version number in the project files match. Otherwise you can get into a situation where pyproject.toml says 1 version and the git tag says a different version.

What I like about the pbr method is for tagged commits it uses the tag version. For versions after the last tagged commit it auto increments a dev version like:

- tag: 1.0.0
- tag+1: 1.0.0-dev0
- tag+2: 1.0.0-dev1
- tag: 2.0.0
- etc...

This way every single commit has a unique version number that's automatically created from previous tagged releases. No one needs to remember to bump the version in any files and release automation can happen through pushes of git tags.

Why do we need it?

It is. Marking the dataclass as frozen means that dataclass with autogenerate __hash__ and __eq__ for us: it's the spiritual equivalent of `#[derive(Hash, PartialEq)]`. 

For the __hash__ to actually work at runtime, every field needs to be hashable. We need to use FrozenDict instead of `dict` - `dict` isn't hashable because it's mutable. Same with `tuple` instead of `list`.
